text:
GENITOURINARY ONCOLOGY — FOLLOW-UP VISIT
Metastatic Clear Cell Renal Cell Carcinoma (cT3bN2M1, Stage IV)
Second-Line Therapy: Cabozantinib (Cabometyx) — Approximately 9 Months on Current Regimen

---

CHIEF COMPLAINT
Mrs. Margaret Okonkwo is a 64-year-old woman with known metastatic clear cell renal cell carcinoma (ccRCC) with sarcomatoid features who presents for routine oncology follow-up on cabozantinib 40 mg orally once daily. She reports stable overall condition. The primary new concern today is mild right hip discomfort that has developed over the past several weeks.

---

HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS
Mrs. Okonkwo is well known to our service. She was diagnosed approximately 18 months ago with IMDC intermediate-risk metastatic ccRCC originating from an 11.4 cm right renal primary with involvement of the right renal vein and infrarenal IVC (level I/II thrombus), retroperitoneal lymphadenopathy (aortocaval and right para-aortic), and bilateral pulmonary metastases. Pathology demonstrated WHO-ISUP grade 4, sarcomatoid differentiation in approximately 22% of the tumor volume, and high PD-L1 expression (CPS ~55 by 22C3 IHC). Comprehensive genomic profiling revealed VHL nonsense mutation, PBRM1 splice site loss-of-function, SETD2 frameshift, and CDKN2A homozygous deletion, consistent with an aggressive genomic profile.

She underwent cytoreductive right radical nephrectomy with retroperitoneal lymph node dissection and IVC thrombectomy without perioperative complications of note. Final surgical pathology confirmed pT3bN2 disease (9 of 14 lymph nodes involved, largest 3.1 cm with extranodal extension).

Following surgical recovery, she initiated first-line ipilimumab (Yervoy) 1 mg/kg IV every 3 weeks plus nivolumab (Opdivo) 3 mg/kg IV every 3 weeks for four induction cycles, transitioning to nivolumab 480 mg IV every 4 weeks for maintenance, per the CheckMate 214 regimen. During induction, she developed grade 2 immune-related colitis (managed with oral budesonide, one ipilimumab dose held) and later grade 2 immune checkpoint inhibitor-associated hypothyroidism (TSH 18.4 mIU/L; levothyroxine initiated, now at 100 mcg/day with stable thyroid function). She achieved a partial response at the first restaging scan (~12 weeks), with all four bilateral pulmonary nodules shrinking substantially. At the second restaging (~6 months), however, she developed a mixed response: the right lower lobe nodule enlarged significantly while the other pulmonary lesions remained stable, and a new 1.3 cm right adrenal nodule appeared. CT-guided biopsy of the adrenal nodule confirmed metastatic ccRCC.

An MDT assessment recommended oligoprogression-directed SBRT to the right lower lobe pulmonary lesion (54 Gy in 3 fractions) while nivolumab was continued. However, her third restaging scan at approximately 9 months demonstrated systemic progressive disease: multiple pulmonary nodules enlarging, a new 1.6 cm segment VI hepatic lesion, and slight adrenal progression. She was therefore transitioned to second-line cabozantinib (Cabometyx) 60 mg daily, with a prompt dose reduction to 40 mg daily within 3 weeks due to grade 2 palmar-plantar erythrodysesthesia (hand-foot syndrome) and grade 2 diarrhea. Additionally, she developed grade 2 hypertension approximately 6 weeks into therapy, managed with up-titration of amlodipine and addition of lisinopril 10 mg daily; blood pressure has since been well controlled.

Her most recent restaging CT, performed at the 6-month mark on cabozantinib, demonstrated continued stable disease with minor decrements in all target lesions (sum of diameters 6.1 cm, essentially unchanged from the 6.2 cm observed at first restaging on this agent). She has now been on cabozantinib for approximately 9 months at the 40 mg/day dose.

Today, she presents reporting a new complaint of mild right hip discomfort, present for approximately 3-4 weeks. The pain is described as a dull ache, predominantly involving the lateral right hip and proximal thigh region, worst with prolonged walking or standing. There is no radiation down the leg, no associated weakness or neurologic symptoms, and no history of trauma. She denies back pain, rib pain, or bone pain elsewhere. Given her underlying metastatic RCC and the fact that osseous metastases from RCC are well recognized and can be painful, a bone scan was ordered at her prior visit to evaluate for this possibility. The result of that bone scan is being reviewed today.

She also provides an interval update: she has maintained her ability to participate in light daily activities including short walks with her daughter and spending time with her grandchildren. She reports her overall energy level as acceptable, noting persistent but tolerable fatigue. No new rashes, no fevers, no night sweats, no weight loss over the past 3 months. She denies dyspnea at rest; mild exertional dyspnea with brisk walking present but not significantly changed from prior visits. No hemoptysis, no chest pain. Appetite is fair to good. Bowel habits: 1-2 loose stools above baseline per day, unchanged and manageable without regular loperamide use. She reports occasional use of loperamide (roughly twice weekly) for days with more diarrhea.

She is tolerating her medications overall. She has not missed any cabozantinib doses in the past several weeks and denies any self-initiated dose holds.

---

REVIEW OF SYSTEMS
Constitutional: Persistent grade 1 fatigue, present and stable. No fever. No chills. No unintentional weight loss. No night sweats.
Head/Eyes/Ears/Nose/Throat: No headaches, no visual changes, no tinnitus.
Cardiovascular: No chest pain, no palpitations, no lower extremity edema.
Respiratory: Mild exertional dyspnea (unchanged). No cough. No hemoptysis. No pleuritic chest pain.
Gastrointestinal: Mild diarrhea 1-2 stools/day above baseline (grade 1, unchanged, managed with dietary modification and occasional PRN loperamide). No nausea or vomiting at present. No abdominal pain. No blood in stool.
Genitourinary: Voiding without difficulty. No hematuria. No dysuria (single kidney, monitored).
Musculoskeletal: Right hip ache as noted, lateral hip and proximal thigh. No other joint complaints. No arthralgia. No myalgia beyond mild generalized fatigue.
Neurologic: No headaches, no focal deficits, no seizures, no cognitive changes.
Skin: Residual grade 1 hand-foot syndrome: mild dryness and callusing of bilateral palms and soles, worst at right heel. No active blistering or open ulceration. No new rash.
Endocrine: No cold or heat intolerance. No palpitations (on levothyroxine, thyroid function stable). No excessive sweating.
Psychiatric: She describes her mood as generally good. She is focused on maintaining quality of life and being present for her family.

---

PHYSICAL EXAMINATION
Vital Signs: Blood pressure 134/82 mmHg (at goal; baseline hypertension managed with amlodipine 10 mg and lisinopril 10 mg), heart rate 72 bpm, respiratory rate 16/min, temperature 36.8°C, oxygen saturation 96% on room air, weight 68.4 kg (stable from prior visit).
Performance Status: ECOG 1.
General: Well-appearing, well-nourished woman in no acute distress. Alert and oriented. Conversant and pleasant.
Head/Neck: Normocephalic, atraumatic. Sclerae anicteric. Mucous membranes moist. No thyromegaly or thyroid nodules on palpation. No cervical or supraclavicular lymphadenopathy.
Cardiovascular: Regular rate and rhythm. Normal S1/S2. No murmurs, rubs, or gallops. No lower extremity pitting edema bilaterally.
Respiratory: Clear to auscultation bilaterally. No wheezes, rales, or rhonchi. No dullness to percussion. Effort adequate.
Abdomen: Soft, non-tender, non-distended. Well-healed right-sided midline laparotomy scar from prior cytoreductive nephrectomy. No palpable hepatosplenomegaly. No ascites. No palpable abdominal mass.
Musculoskeletal: Right hip examination: mild tenderness to palpation over the lateral right hip/greater trochanter region. No tenderness over the iliac crest, sacrum, or right femoral shaft. Internal and external hip rotation preserved and elicited no significant pain (consistent with trochanteric-region source rather than intra-articular hip joint). No leg length discrepancy. No groin tenderness. Left hip: non-tender, full range of motion. Spine: no midline or paraspinal tenderness. No costovertebral angle tenderness bilaterally.
Skin: Bilateral palms and soles with mild hyperkeratosis and fissuring, predominantly right heel (grade 1 hand-foot syndrome). No active erosions, ulceration, or blistering. No new skin lesions, rash, or purpura.
Neurologic: Cranial nerves II-XII grossly intact. Motor strength 5/5 in all extremities. Sensation intact to light touch. Gait: slightly antalgic favoring right side, but ambulates without assistive device. No focal deficits.
Lymphatics: No palpable peripheral lymphadenopathy in axillary, inguinal, or popliteal regions.

---

LABORATORY RESULTS
Complete Blood Count:
  - WBC: 5.4 x10³/µL (normal)
  - Hemoglobin: 10.2 g/dL (mildly low; stable, consistent with prior values; anemia of chronic disease/single kidney, no transfusion threshold reached)
  - MCV: 88 fL (normocytic)
  - Platelets: 198,000/µL (normal)

Comprehensive Metabolic Panel:
  - Sodium: 138 mEq/L; Potassium: 4.1 mEq/L; Chloride: 103 mEq/L; CO2: 25 mEq/L
  - BUN: 22 mg/dL; Creatinine: 1.2 mg/dL (stable, appropriate for solitary kidney post right radical nephrectomy); eGFR: ~56 mL/min/1.73m² (CKD stage 3a, stable)
  - Glucose: 94 mg/dL (normal)
  - ALT: 28 U/L (normal); AST: 31 U/L (normal); Alkaline phosphatase: 108 U/L (borderline elevated, stable; previously elevated in setting of hepatic metastasis)
  - Total bilirubin: 0.7 mg/dL; Albumin: 3.7 g/dL (adequate nutritional status)
  - Calcium: 9.3 mg/dL (normal; important to monitor in the setting of bone metastasis concern)

Thyroid Function:
  - TSH: 2.5 mIU/L (within normal range; therapeutic on levothyroxine 100 mcg/day; previously as high as 18.4 mIU/L prior to supplementation)

Lipid Panel (ordered given statin therapy): Within acceptable range on statin; not detailed here.

Urinalysis: Trace proteinuria (1+), unchanged from prior measurement; 24-hour urine protein was 680 mg at last assessment (below the threshold for cabozantinib dose modification per protocol).

---

IMAGING / BONE SCAN RESULTS
Whole-Body Bone Scintigraphy (Tc-99m MDP): NEGATIVE FOR OSSEOUS METASTATIC DISEASE. No focal areas of abnormal tracer uptake in the spine, pelvis, femur, or elsewhere to suggest skeletal metastases. Incidental finding: mildly increased uptake at the right hip consistent with degenerative/osteoarthritic change, corresponding clinically to her right hip pain. No rib lesions. No sacral or pelvic cortical destruction.

Most Recent Restaging CT (performed at 6-month cabozantinib mark, reviewed for reference): Stable disease maintained. Target lesion sum 6.1 cm. No new sites of disease at that assessment. Next formal restaging CT is scheduled for approximately 3 months from today.

---

ASSESSMENT AND PLAN

1. Metastatic Clear Cell Renal Cell Carcinoma (cT3bN2M1, Stage IV) — Currently on Second-Line Cabozantinib 40 mg Daily, Ongoing Stable Disease

Mrs. Okonkwo has now been on cabozantinib for approximately 9 months since the documented systemic progression on first-line ipilimumab/nivolumab. Her most recent restaging imaging at 6 months confirmed continued stable disease, with minor tumor decrements across all measured sites: bilateral pulmonary nodules, the previously SBRT-treated right lower lobe lesion, the segment VI hepatic metastasis, and the right adrenal metastasis. This represents a durable disease stabilization, which is a meaningful clinical benefit in this population, particularly given the aggressive features of her primary tumor (sarcomatoid differentiation 22%, CDKN2A homozygous deletion, PBRM1 loss, SETD2 frameshift, bulky retroperitoneal nodal disease at diagnosis).

The overall trajectory is gratifying. She has tolerated a prolonged course of TKI therapy with manageable toxicities, and her performance status has remained ECOG 1 throughout. The patient continues to derive clinical benefit and maintains functional independence.

Plan: Continue cabozantinib 40 mg orally once daily. No dose escalation indicated given prior grade 2 toxicity at 60 mg. Repeat restaging CT chest/abdomen/pelvis with contrast in approximately 3 months. Will consider adding MRI brain to the next restaging evaluation given duration of systemic therapy and prior brain MRI having been performed only at initial staging and then once during treatment.

2. New Right Hip Discomfort — Osteoarthritis (Confirmed; Osseous Metastasis Excluded)

The bone scan obtained at the prior visit is today reviewed and is reassuringly negative for osseous metastatic disease. The pattern of uptake is consistent with mild osteoarthritis at the right hip, correlating well with the lateral hip/greater trochanteric distribution of her pain and the lack of true hip joint restriction on examination. This is consistent with degenerative disease expected in a 64-year-old woman.

Plan: No further oncologic workup needed for this complaint at this time. Reassure patient that bone scan is negative for cancer involvement. Recommend NSAIDs with caution given single kidney and ongoing antihypertensive therapy (prefer acetaminophen 650 mg TID for analgesic effect in the short term; avoid NSAIDs if possible). Orthopedic or physiatry referral discussed; patient will trial conservative management first with supportive measures. Silicone trochanteric pad/hip support for lateral hip pain. Revisit at next visit. If pain significantly worsens or new neurologic symptoms develop, orthopedic imaging (X-ray right hip) and repeat bone assessment would be appropriate.

3. Palmar-Plantar Erythrodysesthesia (Hand-Foot Syndrome) — Grade 1, Stable

Her hand-foot syndrome has stabilized at grade 1 since the dose reduction to 40 mg. Right heel remains the most symptomatic site. There is no active blistering or ulceration. She is using urea cream (40%) to bilateral soles twice daily, silicone heel cups in her shoes, and avoids prolonged standing and heat exposure. Emollient cream to hands nightly.

Plan: Continue current supportive care regimen. No dose modification indicated at this time for grade 1 HFS. Dermatology follow-up as needed if worsening. Reinforce foot inspection daily (particularly important given possible decreased sensation at areas of callusing).

4. Diarrhea — Grade 1, Stable

Ongoing 1-2 loose stools above baseline per day, consistent with prior visits and well within the grade 1 range. Managed with dietary fiber modification (BRAT diet elements as needed, avoidance of high-fat and spicy foods), adequate hydration, and occasional PRN loperamide (roughly twice weekly). No weight loss related to diarrhea. Electrolytes normal. No dehydration or significant cramping.

Plan: Continue current approach. No dose modification for grade 1 diarrhea. Educate patient to contact clinic if diarrhea escalates to ≥4-6 stools/day above baseline (which would trigger dose hold per protocol).

5. Hypothyroidism — Stable on Levothyroxine 100 mcg/day

TSH 2.5 mIU/L, within therapeutic range. This represents checkpoint inhibitor-associated hypothyroidism that developed during nivolumab maintenance and required levothyroxine supplementation. Symptomatically, she has no recurrence of fatigue attributable to hypothyroidism as distinct from disease/treatment fatigue. Cabozantinib can also contribute to hypothyroidism or worsen existing disease, and monitoring has appropriately been maintained.

Plan: Continue levothyroxine 100 mcg/day. Repeat TSH in 3 months at next visit. No change at this time.

6. Hypertension — At Goal

Blood pressure 134/82 mmHg today, meeting our target of <140/90 mmHg (and ideally <130/80 given single kidney with trace proteinuria and ongoing lisinopril use). On amlodipine 10 mg daily and lisinopril 10 mg daily. Potassium 4.1 mEq/L (normal; important to monitor on RAAS inhibitor).

Plan: Continue current antihypertensive regimen unchanged. Reinforce home blood pressure monitoring (patient has home cuff). Repeat creatinine and electrolytes at next visit.

7. Chronic Kidney Disease, Stage 3a (Single Kidney)

Creatinine stable at 1.2 mg/dL; eGFR ~56 mL/min/1.73m². This is expected and appropriate for a solitary kidney following right radical nephrectomy. Trace proteinuria present (1+), with prior 24-hour urine protein 680 mg/day (below the 2 g/day threshold for cabozantinib dose modification). Lisinopril provides nephroprotection.

Plan: Monitor creatinine, BUN, and urinalysis at each visit. Avoid nephrotoxic medications (NSAIDs, IV contrast when alternative imaging is feasible). Nephrology co-management to be considered if eGFR deteriorates further.

8. Mild Anemia (Anemia of Chronic Disease / Cancer)

Hemoglobin 10.2 g/dL, stable and above transfusion threshold. MCV normocytic. No evidence of iron deficiency or vitamin deficiency at this time. This is a chronic, expected finding in the setting of metastatic malignancy and prior surgery.

Plan: Monitor closely. If symptomatic anemia develops (dyspnea at rest, significant fatigue, lightheadedness), consider further workup (iron studies, B12/folate, reticulocyte count) and potential erythropoiesis-stimulating agent discussion in accordance with ASCO guidelines. No intervention today.

9. Goals of Care and Psychosocial Support

At our prior visit, we had an initial goals-of-care conversation and arranged integration with palliative care. The patient has articulated clearly that her priorities are quality of life, maintaining independence, and being present for meaningful family events (grandchildren, family gatherings). She is living with her adult daughter, who has been a supportive caregiver. She appears emotionally adjusted and engaged in her care. The palliative care team has been co-managing her symptom burden, particularly fatigue and the psychological aspects of living with metastatic cancer.

Plan: Maintain ongoing dialogue about goals of care at each visit. Palliative care co-management to continue. Social work follow-up. If disease progresses on cabozantinib in the future, we will discuss available options including enrollment in clinical trials, belzutifan (HIF-2α inhibitor, approved third-line after IO and TKI), everolimus-based combinations, and other investigational strategies—but that conversation is premature given current stability.

---

MEDICATIONS (Current):
1. Cabozantinib (Cabometyx) 40 mg orally once daily (dose-reduced from 60 mg due to prior grade 2 HFS and diarrhea)
2. Levothyroxine (Synthroid) 100 mcg orally once daily (for ICI-associated hypothyroidism)
3. Amlodipine 10 mg orally once daily (hypertension, pre-existing)
4. Lisinopril 10 mg orally once daily (added for cabozantinib-associated hypertension; also nephroprotective)
5. Atorvastatin (dose per prior prescription; hyperlipidemia, pre-existing)
6. Urea 40% cream topically to bilateral soles twice daily (HFS management)
7. Loperamide 2-4 mg orally PRN (grade 1 diarrhea; used approximately twice weekly)
8. Acetaminophen 650 mg orally TID PRN (right hip pain, osteoarthritis)
9. Ondansetron 4 mg orally PRN (nausea; rarely used at this time)

ALLERGIES: No known drug allergies.

---

FOLLOW-UP:
- Return in approximately 3 months for scheduled follow-up and restaging CT chest/abdomen/pelvis with contrast. Consider MRI brain at that restaging given duration of therapy.
- Interim contact: Patient and daughter instructed to call with worsening right hip pain, new bone pain, significant increase in diarrhea (≥6 stools/day above baseline), worsening hand-foot syndrome, shortness of breath, or any new neurologic symptoms.
- Labs at next visit: CBC, CMP, TSH, urinalysis.
- Bone scan result communicated to patient today; reassured of negative findings.

Time spent with patient: 35 minutes. Greater than 50% of visit time devoted to counseling and coordination of care.

Electronically signed,
Attending Physician, Genitourinary Medical Oncology
